enrichment.short_description: Ente Photos is an open-source, end-to-end encrypted photo and video backup and sharing service, built as a privacy-first alternative to Google Photos and iCloud Photos.
enrichment.seo_title: Ente Photos Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ente Photos is an open-source, end-to-end encrypted alternative to Google Photos and iCloud Photos. See pricing tiers, features, security and competitors.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Ente Photos is an open-source, end-to-end encrypted photo and video backup and sharing platform built as a privacy-first alternative to Google Photos and Apple iCloud Photos. Founded in 2020 by Vishnu Mohandas, a former Google engineer who worked on Assistant serving infrastructure, the company was started after Mohandas grew uneasy about trusting large cloud providers with a lifetime of personal photos and videos. Co-founder Neeraj Gupta joined shortly after, leaving a job at Uber to help build a photo backup product that would protect his newborn child's photos from being scanned, mined, or exposed by a third party. The company operates as Ente Technologies, Inc., is headquartered in Bengaluru, India, and has deliberately chosen to remain bootstrapped rather than raise venture capital; Mohandas has said in interviews that the company does not want outside investors dictating how user data is handled. By mid 2026 the team had grown from roughly a dozen employees in early 2025 to around 68 people, most of whom came from the open-source community, working across engineering, design, support, and product.

Technically, Ente is built as a monorepo (github.com/ente-io/ente) containing every client application alongside the server that powers them. The mobile apps for iOS and Android are written in Dart using Flutter, which lets the team ship one codebase to both platforms plus an F-Droid build for users who avoid Google Play services. The web client and desktop apps for Windows, macOS, and Linux are built with TypeScript, and the backend server is written in Go. Smaller portions of the codebase use Rust for supporting tooling and native Swift and Kotlin for platform-specific integrations. The entire monorepo, both the server and every client, is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPL-3.0), which requires that anyone who runs a modified version of Ente as a hosted service must publish their changes, keeping the code that handles user data permanently inspectable. This AGPL licensing also enables a genuine self-hosting option: technically inclined users can deploy their own Ente server and point the official apps at it instead of Ente's hosted infrastructure.

Ente's core technical claim is true end-to-end encryption, not just encryption in transit or at rest on the server. Every photo, video, thumbnail, and piece of metadata is encrypted on the user's device using the libsodium cryptography library before it is ever uploaded. Ente uses XChaCha20-Poly1305 authenticated encryption for file contents (via libsodium's crypto_secretstream APIs), Argon2id for password-based key derivation with high memory and computation cost parameters, and 256-bit keys generated with crypto_secretbox_keygen for the user's master key, recovery key, collection keys, and individual file keys. When a user signs up, the client generates a master key that never leaves the device in unencrypted form; a key-encryption-key derived from the user's password encrypts that master key before the encrypted version is sent to Ente's servers, meaning Ente itself cannot decrypt user photos even if compelled to or breached. This architecture has been externally audited by three independent security firms: Cure53 (a German cybersecurity firm), Symbolic Software (French cryptography specialists), and Fallible (an Indian penetration testing firm), and Ente publishes its audit reports for public review.

Beyond encrypted storage and sync, Ente Photos includes encrypted sharing and collaborative albums, so users can share individual photos or entire albums via links that remain end-to-end encrypted, and invite collaborators to add photos to a shared album without granting them access to the rest of the library. The app also includes on-device machine learning for face recognition and a semantic 'magic search' powered by a locally run CLIP model; both features run entirely on the user's device, so face data, search queries, and AI-generated indexes never reach Ente's servers in unencrypted form, and Ente states it does not use customer photos or derived data to train any AI models. Family plans let a subscriber share their storage quota with up to five additional family members at no extra cost, each with their own encrypted, private space within the shared quota.

On pricing, Ente offers a free plan with 10 GB of storage that does not expire, provided the account is used at least once a year to stay active. Paid tiers scale by storage: roughly 50 GB for about 2.49 to 2.99 dollars per month (cheaper when billed annually), 200 GB for about 4.99 dollars per month, 1 TB for about 9.99 dollars per month, and 2 TB for about 19.99 dollars per month, with annual billing offering a discount over paying monthly. Every paid plan can be shared with up to five family members under the family plan feature, and Ente periodically runs referral programs that grant additional free storage when a referred user upgrades to a paid plan. Alongside Ente Photos, the company also ships Ente Auth, a free, open-source two-factor authentication app with encrypted cloud backups, and Ente Locker, a freemium encrypted file and document vault, both of which reuse the same end-to-end encrypted sync infrastructure as Photos.

As of mid-2026, Ente reports more than 150,000 registered customers who have collectively stored well over 165 million photos, and its GitHub repository has accumulated roughly 27,700 stars, making it one of the most visible self-hosted photo storage projects on the platform. The company was covered by TechCrunch in April 2025 in an article framing Ente as a serious, privacy-first challenger to Google Photos, and it has been reviewed favorably by privacy-focused outlets such as PrivacyTools.io and independent bloggers who have compared it directly to mainstream cloud photo services. Ente's most direct competitors fall into two camps: mainstream, closed-source incumbents like Google Photos, Apple iCloud Photos, and Dropbox, which offer convenience and deep platform integration but do not offer true end-to-end encryption or open-source transparency; and other self-hosted or privacy-oriented alternatives such as Immich, PhotoPrism, and Nextcloud Photos, which appeal to the same technically sophisticated audience but generally require users to manage their own server and, in most cases, do not implement end-to-end encryption of the underlying media files by default.

Ente differentiates itself from the self-hosted competition primarily through its hosted, consumer-friendly product: users who do not want to run their own server can simply subscribe and get a polished, cross-platform app with the same end-to-end encryption guarantees as the self-hosted deployment, because the hosted and self-hosted paths share the exact same open-source, audited codebase. This dual approach, a maintained commercial service built entirely on inspectable, AGPL-licensed code, is central to Ente's pitch to privacy-conscious individuals, families, journalists, and organizations that want the convenience of automatic cloud backup without having to trust a provider with unencrypted access to their photos. The target user ranges from individual privacy enthusiasts migrating away from Google Photos or iCloud, to families who want to pool storage and share albums securely, to self-hosters and open-source contributors who want full control over where their data physically lives. Ente also emphasizes longevity as a design goal: because the full stack is open source under AGPL-3.0, the company has stated publicly that it wants to build something that could outlive its founders, since the community can always fork and continue operating the service even if the original company were to shut down, a guarantee that closed-source incumbents structurally cannot offer their users. Ente's continued bootstrapped growth, its published third-party security audits, and its steadily expanding feature set around on-device AI search and family sharing all point to a company positioning itself as the long-term, trustworthy alternative to Big Tech photo storage rather than a short-lived venture-backed experiment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ente Photos is an open-source, end-to-end encrypted cloud backup and sharing service for photos and videos. It is built by Ente Technologies, Inc., a bootstrapped company founded in 2020 by Vishnu Mohandas and headquartered in Bengaluru, India, and it is positioned as a privacy-respecting alternative to Google Photos and Apple iCloud Photos.

The entire product, mobile apps, desktop apps, web client, and the Go-based server, lives in a single open-source monorepo licensed under AGPL-3.0. That licensing means anyone can audit the code that handles their photos, and technically capable users can self-host their own Ente server rather than using Ente's hosted infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the core of Ente Photos is client-side, end-to-end encryption powered by libsodium: files are encrypted with XChaCha20-Poly1305 before they ever leave the device, and passwords are strengthened with Argon2id key derivation, so Ente's servers never see unencrypted photos, videos, or metadata.

Beyond storage, Ente supports encrypted collaborative albums and shareable links, family plans that pool storage across up to five members, cross-platform native apps for iOS, Android, F-Droid, Windows, macOS, Linux, and the web, and on-device machine learning for face recognition and CLIP-powered semantic search that never sends photo data off the device.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ente Photos offers a free plan with 10 GB of storage that never expires as long as the account is used at least once a year. Paid storage plans scale from roughly 50 GB up to 2 TB, priced from about 2.49 to 19.99 dollars per month depending on tier, with a discount for annual billing.

Every paid subscription can be shared with up to five family members at no extra cost, and self-hosting the AGPL-3.0 server is free for anyone willing to run their own infrastructure instead of subscribing to Ente's hosted service.

faqs.question: Is Ente Photos really end-to-end encrypted?
faqs.answer: Yes. Files are encrypted on the user's device with libsodium (XChaCha20-Poly1305 and Argon2id key derivation) before upload, and the encryption keys are derived from the user's password, so Ente's servers never have access to unencrypted photos or videos.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Ente Photos?
faqs.answer: Yes. The full server and client codebase is open source under AGPL-3.0 on GitHub, so technically capable users can run their own Ente server and connect the official apps to it instead of using Ente's hosted service.

faqs.question: What does the free plan include?
faqs.answer: Ente offers a free plan with 10 GB of storage that remains active as long as the account is used at least once a year.

faqs.question: Does Ente Photos have a family plan?
faqs.answer: Yes. Any paid subscription can be shared with up to five additional family members at no extra cost, with each member getting their own encrypted, private space within the pooled storage.

faqs.question: How is Ente Photos different from Google Photos or iCloud Photos?
faqs.answer: Unlike Google Photos and iCloud Photos, Ente encrypts content end-to-end on the device before upload, meaning Ente itself cannot view user photos, and its entire codebase is open source and independently audited rather than closed and proprietary.

faqs.question: How does Ente Photos compare to Immich, PhotoPrism, or Nextcloud?
faqs.answer: Immich, PhotoPrism, and Nextcloud are self-hosted photo tools that generally require users to manage their own server and typically do not encrypt media end-to-end by default. Ente offers both a polished hosted subscription and a genuine self-hosting option, with end-to-end encryption applied in either case.

enrichment.short_description: Eonvelope is an open-source, self-hosted email archiving application that continuously fetches mail over IMAP, POP, Exchange, and JMAP to preserve it long-term with full-text search.
enrichment.seo_title: Eonvelope Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Eonvelope is a free, self-hosted, open-source email archive built on Django. See its features, Docker setup, licensing, and how it compares to alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Eonvelope is an open-source, self-hostable email archiving application built by developer David Aderbauer (GitLab handle Dacid99) and community contributors. Its stated purpose is to let individuals and organizations preserve their email indefinitely by continuously and automatically fetching messages from IMAP, POP, Microsoft Exchange, and JMAP accounts into a searchable, self-controlled archive, rather than relying on a mail provider to retain history.

Technically, the project is a Django (Python) web application, with the GitHub language breakdown showing the codebase is roughly 81 percent Python and the remainder CSS, HTML, and a small amount of JavaScript. It ships as a container-based deployment, distributed via Docker images on Docker Hub with 'latest' tag tracking, and the documentation also describes Podman and Kubernetes deployment paths. The web interface is Bootstrap5-based and mobile-friendly with PWA support, and the project exposes a REST API alongside full-text search, filtering, and correspondent/thread management. The maintainers describe a 'slim mode' aimed at low-spec systems and cite over 95 percent automated test coverage.

The project is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License v3.0 or later (AGPLv3), with documentation separately licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0, both of which are standard copyleft choices for self-hosted, community-run open-source software. Primary development happens on GitLab (gitlab.com/Dacid99/eonvelope), with a read-only mirror on GitHub that, as of this review, shows 222 stars, 9 forks, and 18 tagged releases, the most recent being version 0.7.2. The GitHub mirror repository was created in June 2025, marking the project's earliest verifiable public footprint. Documentation is hosted on ReadTheDocs and the project accepts community translations via Weblate, indicating an actively maintained, community-oriented open-source effort rather than a commercial product. There is no evidence of a company behind the project, employee count, headquarters, or external funding; it appears to be maintained as a personal/community open-source project, optionally supported through a 'Buy Me a Coffee' donation link.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Eonvelope is a self-hosted, open-source email archiving tool. Rather than a SaaS product with a company behind it, it is a community-maintained project whose primary repository lives on GitLab under the account Dacid99 (David Aderbauer), with a read-only mirror on GitHub.

The tool's core job is to continuously and automatically pull mail from IMAP, POP, Microsoft Exchange, and JMAP accounts and store it in a searchable local archive that the user fully controls, rather than depending on the mailbox provider to retain that history.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Eonvelope is built on Django (Python) with a Bootstrap5-based, mobile-friendly progressive web app (PWA) interface, and exposes a REST API for programmatic access to archived mail.

It supports import and export of email in various formats, email threading and correspondent management, full-text search and filtering, and integrations mentioned in project documentation with tools like Paperless-ngx, Searxng, Grafana, and Immich. A 'slim mode' targets low-spec self-hosting environments, and the project reports over 95 percent automated test coverage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Eonvelope is free and open source, licensed under AGPLv3 (documentation under CC BY-SA 4.0). There is no paid tier; the software is distributed as Docker images and deployed by the user on their own infrastructure (Docker, Podman, or Kubernetes).
The only monetary element visible on the project's public pages is an optional 'Buy Me a Coffee' donation link for supporting ongoing development, not a purchasable plan.

faqs.question: What is Eonvelope?
faqs.answer: Eonvelope is an open-source, self-hosted application for archiving and preserving email over the long term by continuously fetching messages from IMAP, POP, Exchange, or JMAP accounts.

faqs.question: Is Eonvelope free?
faqs.answer: Yes. Eonvelope is free and open source under the AGPLv3 license, with no paid plans; users self-host it on their own infrastructure.

faqs.question: How do I install Eonvelope?
faqs.answer: Eonvelope is distributed as Docker images and can be deployed via Docker Compose, Podman, or Kubernetes, following the setup documentation on ReadTheDocs.

faqs.question: What email protocols does Eonvelope support?
faqs.answer: It supports IMAP, POP, Microsoft Exchange, and JMAP for fetching and archiving mail.

faqs.question: Who maintains Eonvelope?
faqs.answer: It is maintained by developer David Aderbauer (GitLab account Dacid99) and community contributors, with primary development on GitLab and a read-only mirror on GitHub.

faqs.question: Does Eonvelope have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: There is no dedicated iOS or Android app; Eonvelope offers a mobile-friendly progressive web app (PWA) interface instead.

enrichment.short_description: EPrints is a free, open-source software platform for building institutional repositories and open access archives, developed at the University of Southampton School of Electronics and Computer Science.
enrichment.seo_title: EPrints Tool Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: EPrints review 2026: open-source institutional repository software from the University of Southampton. Features, GPL licensing, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
EPrints is a free, open-source software platform used by universities, research institutions, and libraries to build institutional repositories, which are online archives that collect, preserve, and provide open access to the scholarly output of an organization, including journal articles, theses, conference papers, datasets, and multimedia research materials. The software was created at the University of Southampton School of Electronics and Computer Science (ECS) in the United Kingdom and released in 2000, making it one of the very earliest institutional repository platforms in the world, predating most of its later competitors.

EPrints traces its lineage to Professor Stevan Harnad, widely regarded as one of the founders of the Open Access movement and known for his 1994 'Subversive Proposal' calling for researchers to self-archive their peer-reviewed papers openly online. Harnad's earlier CogPrints archive for cognitive science papers served as a proof of concept, and the idea was generalized into a piece of software that any institution could install for free to run its own open archive. This generalization effort was carried out with support from the UK's Joint Information Systems Committee (JISC) through the Open Citation Project, a collaboration between the University of Southampton, Cornell University, and arXiv. The first version of the EPrints software was developed primarily by Robert Tansley and released in 2000. Dr. Leslie (Les) Carr subsequently became the long-standing Technical Director of the open-source EPrints project, guiding its development at Southampton for many years. The timing of EPrints' creation coincided closely with the 1999 Santa Fe meeting that produced the Open Archives Initiative Protocol for Metadata Harvesting (OAI-PMH), and EPrints was built from the outset to be compliant with that protocol, allowing repositories built on it to be harvested and indexed by search services, aggregators, and other repositories worldwide.

Technically, EPrints is written in Perl and runs as a LAMP-style web application, historically deployed on Linux and Solaris, with Mac OS X support added early on and native Microsoft Windows support introduced in May 2010. The software is distributed under the GNU General Public License (GPL-3.0-or-later), meaning institutions can download, install, modify, and redistribute it without licensing fees, and are free to inspect and adapt the source code to their local needs. A major architectural milestone came with the release of EPrints version 3 on January 24, 2007, unveiled at the Open Repositories 2007 conference. Version 3 introduced a plugin architecture for data import and export, giving repository managers far more flexibility to customize workflows, user interfaces, and metadata handling than the original version 1 and 2 releases allowed. Configuration in EPrints is handled through a combination of Perl and XML files, while the user-facing templates are built with HTML, CSS, and images, giving technically capable institutional IT teams substantial control over look, feel, and behavior. The software also supports internationalization through XML phrase files, with translations available in languages including French, German, Spanish, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Hungarian, Bulgarian, and Ukrainian, reflecting its adoption well beyond the English-speaking academic world. Development has continued for over two decades, with the codebase maintained publicly on GitHub under the eprints organization (eprints/eprints3.4), and recent maintenance releases such as version 3.4.7 continuing to ship bug fixes and compatibility updates.

EPrints operates on an open-source, self-hosted pricing model: the core software itself is free to download and run, and there is no vendor lock-in fee for the code. In practice, institutions either self-host EPrints on their own servers using in-house technical staff, or they contract with EPrints Services, a support and hosting organization based at the University of Southampton, which offers managed hosting, migration, customization, training, and ongoing technical support for a fee. This mirrors the commercial-support-around-free-core model common among open-source academic infrastructure projects. EPrints Services markets itself as having operated for roughly two decades, offering repository solutions tailored to research communities, funder compliance requirements (such as green Open Access mandates), and specialized use cases; for example, the University for the Creative Arts used EPrints to build what is described as the first specialist arts institutional repository, launched in 2010, with tailored metadata fields and visual showcase capabilities suited to art and design outputs rather than only text-based scholarly articles.

The target users of EPrints are primarily university libraries, research offices, and IT departments responsible for managing institutional scholarly output, along with government research bodies, scientific journals, and cultural or arts institutions that need to archive and expose collections in an OAI-PMH-compliant way. Because the software requires server administration and Perl familiarity to install, configure, and maintain, EPrints tends to appeal to institutions with in-house technical capacity or those willing to pay for managed hosting through EPrints Services or similar partners, rather than to individual researchers or small organizations without IT support.

In the institutional repository software space, EPrints' most direct competitors are DSpace, Fedora Commons, Invenio, and Islandora. DSpace, originally developed by MIT Libraries and Hewlett-Packard and released in 2002, is arguably EPrints' closest rival in scale of adoption; it is Java-based rather than Perl-based and has historically had a larger installed base among large research universities. Fedora Commons (now often used as a back-end repository framework rather than a full turnkey product, frequently paired with front ends like Islandora or Samvera) targets more complex digital object and preservation use cases. Invenio, developed at CERN, grew out of large-scale physics document management and is used by scientific organizations with heavy metadata and integration needs. Islandora is a Drupal-based front end typically layered on top of Fedora for digital collections and archives. Compared with these, EPrints is often characterized as lighter-weight and comparatively straightforward to install and configure, which made it attractive to smaller and mid-sized institutions in the 2000s and 2010s, even as some larger research-intensive universities gravitated toward DSpace or Fedora-based stacks for greater scalability and ecosystem integration.

Historically, EPrints holds a notable claim as one of the first, if not the first, purpose-built institutional repository software packages, predating DSpace by roughly two years and helping to establish the technical and policy template that the broader open access repository movement subsequently followed. According to figures published on the EPrints project's own site, the platform has accumulated hundreds of registered repository installations and well over one hundred thousand software downloads globally over its lifetime, with adopters spanning universities such as the University of East Anglia and the University of Glasgow in the UK, among many others internationally. The Directory of Open Access Repositories (OpenDOAR) and the Registry of Open Access Repositories (ROAR), both also associated with the University of Southampton's open access research community, have long tracked EPrints installations as part of the broader global census of institutional repositories.

In terms of product evolution, EPrints has moved from its original Perl/CGI-driven version 1 architecture through the plugin-based version 3 released in 2007, continuing to add features over time such as improved usage statistics and reporting, better compliance tooling for funder open access mandates, and broader operating system support. It remains actively maintained as of the mid-2020s, with releases in the 3.4.x branch, and its open-source codebase continues to be developed collaboratively via GitHub, allowing institutions and contributors worldwide to extend, patch, and customize it in line with evolving open access and research data management requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: EPrints is a free, open-source repository platform created at the University of Southampton School of Electronics and Computer Science and first released in 2000. It was one of the earliest software packages purpose-built for institutional open access repositories, letting universities, research bodies, and libraries archive and expose scholarly output such as journal articles, theses, and conference papers in a way that is harvestable via the Open Archives Initiative Protocol for Metadata Harvesting (OAI-PMH).

The project grew out of work by Professor Stevan Harnad, a leading figure in the Open Access movement, and was generalized into installable software by Robert Tansley with support from the UK's Joint Information Systems Committee (JISC). Dr. Leslie Carr later served as the long-standing Technical Director of the open-source project at Southampton.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: EPrints is written in Perl and deploys as a LAMP-style web application, running on Linux, Solaris, Mac OS X, and, since May 2010, native Windows. It is distributed under the GNU General Public License (GPL-3.0-or-later), so institutions can install, modify, and redistribute it without licensing fees.

Version 3, released in January 2007 at the Open Repositories conference, introduced a plugin architecture for data import and export, giving repository managers much finer control over workflows and interfaces. Configuration is handled through Perl and XML, templates use HTML and CSS, and the interface has been translated into languages including French, German, Spanish, Italian, Russian, Japanese, Hungarian, Bulgarian, and Ukrainian.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The EPrints software itself is free and open source, with no licensing cost for downloading, self-hosting, or modifying the codebase. Institutions that prefer not to run and maintain the platform in-house can contract EPrints Services, based at the University of Southampton, for managed hosting, migration, customization, and ongoing technical support on a paid basis.

This free-core, paid-support model is typical of academic open-source infrastructure: smaller institutions with limited IT capacity often pay for hosted support, while larger universities with dedicated technical staff commonly self-host the software directly on their own servers at no licensing cost.

enrichment.short_description: Ergo is an open-source IRC server written in Go that combines an ircd, account services, and bouncer functionality into a single easy-to-configure binary with modern IRCv3 support, message history, and multi-client sync.
enrichment.seo_title: Ergo Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ergo is a free, open-source IRC server written in Go with built-in services, history playback, and IRCv3 support. See features, pricing, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Ergo is an open-source Internet Relay Chat server, commonly called an ircd, written entirely in the Go programming language. What sets it apart from most traditional IRC daemons is that it does not just route messages between connected clients; it bundles together three pieces of infrastructure that IRC operators have historically had to run and maintain separately. It functions as the ircd itself, as an account and channel services framework equivalent to tools like Anope or Atheme through built-in NickServ, ChanServ, and HostServ components, and as a bouncer that stores message history and lets multiple client connections share a single logged-in identity the way standalone bouncer software such as ZNC does. The goal of the project is to let a single administrator stand up a fully featured, persistent, always-on IRC network from one binary and one configuration file, without stitching together several independent daemons.

The project's history traces back to 2012, when Jeremy Latt wrote an early Go-based IRC daemon called Ergonomadic. That codebase lay mostly dormant after 2014 until Daniel Oaks forked it in 2016 to create a new project named Oragono, with the explicit goal of prototyping IRCv3 features and serving as a reference implementation of the community-maintained Modern IRC specification. Under this name the project reached its first stable release, Oragono 1.0.0, in February 2019. Shivaram Lingamneni joined as a core contributor in 2017 and, alongside Daniel Oaks, has driven the majority of the project's development since. In June 2021, coinciding with the v2.7.0 release, the project renamed itself again, this time to Ergo, and moved its home to the ergochat GitHub organization and the ergo.chat domain, retiring the oragono.io site as an archive of the project's earlier era. Understood in full, the naming lineage runs Ergonomadic to Oragono to Ergo, spanning more than a decade of continuous, if occasionally slow-paced, development by a small volunteer team.

Technically, Ergo is distributed as a single compiled Go binary with all of its dependencies vendored at build time, meaning a build does not need to reach out to the network to fetch third-party packages. Configuration is handled through a YAML file that can be rehashed, meaning most settings can be changed and reloaded while the server is running, without dropping existing client connections. Persistent state, including registered accounts, channel registrations, and stored chat history, is kept in an embedded datastore bundled with the server rather than requiring administrators to set up and maintain a separate external database such as PostgreSQL or MySQL. TLS/SSL termination is native to the server, including support for authenticating users via TLS client certificates, so operators do not need to place a reverse proxy in front of Ergo purely to get encrypted connections. For clients that connect through web-based IRC gateways such as Kiwi IRC or The Lounge, Ergo supports the WEBIRC specification, which lets a trusted gateway pass along the real originating IP address and connection security state of the web client rather than having every web user appear to originate from the gateway's own address.

On the protocol side, Ergo positions itself as a reference implementation of IRCv3, the loose set of community specifications that modernize the decades-old IRC protocol with capabilities negotiation, message tags, and richer client behavior. It implements SASL authentication so clients can log into a registered account as part of the connection handshake rather than sending a separate command afterward, supports CHATHISTORY and related mechanisms for replaying missed messages, and allows several devices or client sessions to share one logged-in nickname simultaneously while staying in sync on the same conversation history, which is the essence of its built-in bouncer behavior. It also supports UTF-8 nicknames and channel names per RFC 8265, moving past the ASCII-only restrictions of classic IRC networks. A more niche capability inherited from its Ergonomadic and Oragono ancestry is a set of roleplay commands, including NPC and SCENE, intended for text roleplaying communities that want to send messages attributed to non-player characters; in current releases this feature is deprecated and disabled by default, reflecting a narrowing of scope toward general-purpose chat use rather than roleplay-specific tooling.

Security and abuse-prevention features are built in rather than left to third-party modules. Passwords are stored with bcrypt hashing, administrators can enable IP cloaking to mask users' real addresses in channel views, the server can be configured to run as a Tor hidden service for privacy-focused deployments, and a unified ban system covers classic KLINE and DLINE bans alongside a broader UBAN mechanism, together with ident lookups and connection-rate limiting to slow down abusive clients. LDAP integration is available for operators who want to tie account authentication to an existing directory service rather than Ergo's own account database.

Ergo is released under the MIT license, a short and permissive open-source license that allows free use, modification, and redistribution, including in commercial contexts, with minimal obligations placed on downstream users. The project is hosted publicly on GitHub under the ergochat organization, distributes Docker images through ghcr.io/ergochat/ergo, GPG-signs its release artifacts, and maintains a public test network at testnet.ergo.chat where anyone can try the software without installing it. As of recent releases the project has published more than 75 tagged versions, indicating steady, incremental maintenance rather than either abandonment or venture-style rapid iteration.

The realistic audience for Ergo is IRC network operators and self-hosters who want to run a small-to-medium community network, a private group chat for a team or friend group, or a personal always-on IRC presence, without operating a stack of separate daemons for the ircd, services, and bouncer layers. It also appeals to protocol developers and IRC client authors who want a modern, actively updated server to test IRCv3 features against, since the project explicitly aims to track and help define emerging parts of the Modern IRC specification. It is not aimed at enterprises seeking a support contract, since it is maintained by a small volunteer team rather than a company, and there is no commercial tier, paid support plan, or hosted SaaS offering; the entire cost of running Ergo is the infrastructure the operator provides.

Within the broader IRC daemon landscape, Ergo sits alongside long-established C-language servers such as UnrealIRCd and InspIRCd, both of which are highly configurable and widely deployed but typically expect operators to pair them with separate services packages like Anope or Atheme and separate bouncer software like ZNC to get equivalent functionality. It also sits near daemons descended from the Charybdis codebase, such as Solanum, which powers large federated networks including Libera.Chat, and the older ircd-seven, which historically powered Freenode; these daemons are generally optimized for large-scale, multi-server federated networks rather than the single-server, batteries-included model Ergo targets. Ergo's differentiation is not raw scale but operational simplicity: a Go operator can get a fully featured modern IRC network running from one binary, one config file, and no external services daemon, which is a meaningfully different value proposition than the C-based ircds built for federating dozens of servers across a large public network.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Ergo is a free, open-source IRC server (ircd) written in Go that bundles together an ircd, account and channel services (NickServ, ChanServ, HostServ), and bouncer-style history and multi-client sync into a single binary and configuration file.

Formerly known as Oragono, and before that a fork of the Ergonomadic IRC daemon, Ergo has been developed continuously since 2016 with a focus on modern IRCv3 compliance, ease of deployment, and reducing the number of separate services an operator has to run to stand up a full-featured IRC network.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Ergo ships with integrated services so registered accounts, channel registration, and vanity hosts work out of the box, without installing separate services software. It also functions as a bouncer, storing chat history server-side and letting multiple client sessions share one nickname and see the same conversation history.

On the protocol side, Ergo supports SASL authentication, native TLS/SSL with client certificate support, WEBIRC for web-based gateway clients, UTF-8 nicknames and channel names, and tracks IRCv3 specifications closely enough that it is used as a reference implementation. Security features include bcrypt password hashing, IP cloaking, Tor hidden service support, and a unified ban system covering KLINE, DLINE, and UBAN.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ergo is completely free and open source under the MIT license. There are no paid tiers, license fees, or hosted commercial offering; the only cost to run it is the server infrastructure an operator provides for self-hosting.

Because the project is maintained by a small volunteer team rather than a company, support comes through the project's public GitHub issue tracker and its own IRC community rather than a paid support contract.

faqs.question: What is Ergo?
faqs.answer: Ergo is a free, open-source IRC server written in Go that combines an ircd, account services, and bouncer-style history and multi-client sync into a single binary.

faqs.question: Is Ergo free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ergo is released under the MIT license and is free to download, self-host, and modify, with no paid tiers or license fees.

faqs.question: What was Ergo previously called?
faqs.answer: Ergo was previously named Oragono, which was itself a 2016 fork of an earlier Go-based IRC daemon called Ergonomadic. The project renamed itself to Ergo in June 2021.

faqs.question: Does Ergo support IRCv3?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ergo closely tracks IRCv3 and Modern IRC specifications, including SASL, message tags, and history playback, and is used as a reference implementation of these specifications.

faqs.question: Can Ergo replace separate services software like Anope or Atheme?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ergo includes built-in NickServ, ChanServ, and HostServ services, so operators typically do not need to run separate services software alongside it.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Ergo?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include UnrealIRCd, InspIRCd, ircd-seven, Solanum, and Charybdis, which are established IRC daemons often used for larger, federated networks.

enrichment.short_description: ERPNext is a free, open-source ERP suite covering accounting, inventory, manufacturing, CRM, HR, and project management, built on the Python and JavaScript based Frappe framework by Frappe Technologies.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted, GPLv3); Frappe Cloud managed hosting from $5/month
enrichment.seo_title: ERPNext Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ERPNext review: open-source ERP for accounting, inventory, manufacturing, and CRM. Compare Frappe Cloud pricing, features, and top alternatives like Odoo.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
ERPNext is a full-suite, open-source Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) system that consolidates the core operational functions of a business - accounting, inventory and warehouse management, manufacturing, purchasing, sales, customer relationship management (CRM), human resources and payroll, project management, and point of sale - into a single connected platform. Rather than stitching together separate tools for each department, ERPNext gives a company one shared database and one set of workflows, so a sales order can flow automatically into inventory reservation, a manufacturing work order, an invoice, and a set of accounting entries without manual re-entry. It is distributed under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3), which means the full source code is available on GitHub, can be self-hosted for free, and can be modified or extended by anyone, in contrast to the proprietary, per-seat licensing model used by most enterprise ERP vendors.

The project traces back to Frappe Technologies Pvt Ltd, an Indian software company founded in 2008 by Rushabh Mehta and based in Mumbai. Mehta and his early collaborators originally built a lightweight web application framework to support consulting projects, and that framework eventually became the foundation for a full ERP product. ERPNext itself was first released publicly in June 2010, initially hosted on Google Code before the project migrated to GitHub as that platform became the default home for open-source software. Over the following decade, Frappe Technologies grew ERPNext from a niche accounting and inventory tool into a broad ERP suite used by companies across manufacturing, distribution, retail, healthcare, education, agriculture, and nonprofit sectors, with particularly strong adoption in India and a growing base of implementation partners worldwide. In November 2020, Zerodha's Rainmatter fund - the venture arm of India's largest stockbroker - invested roughly ₹10 crore (about $1.3 million) in Frappe Technologies to support continued development, a rare instance of the company taking outside capital while still preserving its open-source, community-first governance model.

Technically, ERPNext is built on top of the Frappe framework, a Python-based, metadata-driven web application framework that Frappe Technologies also maintains and open-sources independently. The framework's central concept is the 'DocType', a metadata definition that simultaneously describes a database table, a REST API endpoint, a web form, a permission set, and a list/report view. Because business objects like Sales Invoice, Purchase Order, or Employee are defined declaratively as DocTypes rather than hard-coded, developers and even advanced administrators can add custom fields, custom forms, and custom business logic (in Python or JavaScript) without forking the core codebase. The backend runs on Python (with the Frappe framework handling ORM, permissions, background jobs via Redis and RQ, and REST/websocket APIs), the frontend uses a Vue.js-based desk interface, and the primary supported database is MariaDB (with growing PostgreSQL support). Deployments are managed through 'Bench', Frappe's command-line tool for installing, upgrading, and running one or more 'sites' (tenants) on a shared codebase, which is the same mechanism that underpins Frappe Cloud's own multi-tenant hosting infrastructure. This architecture is also why the wider Frappe ecosystem includes sibling applications - such as Frappe HR (HRMS), Frappe CRM, Frappe Helpdesk, Frappe LMS, and Frappe Books - that share the same underlying framework and can be installed alongside ERPNext on one instance.

Pricing for ERPNext has two distinct paths. The first is fully self-hosted and free: because the code is GPLv3-licensed, any organization with technical capacity can download it from GitHub, install it on its own servers or cloud infrastructure, and run it indefinitely without license fees, user caps, or module restrictions. The second path is Frappe Cloud, the company's own managed hosting service at frappe.io/erpnext and cloud.frappe.io, aimed at customers who want ERPNext without operating the infrastructure themselves. Frappe Cloud is priced by compute resource usage rather than by user seat, which is a meaningful differentiator from most SaaS ERP competitors. Its entry-level 'Sites' plan starts at roughly $5 per month and provides a single site on shared servers with managed upgrades, backups, custom domains, SSH access, and one-click installs from a marketplace of 150-plus Frappe-framework apps. A mid-tier 'Servers' (or private bench) plan starts at roughly $20 to $40 per month depending on the current published tier, offering a dedicated virtual machine capable of running multiple sites and benches, server-level analytics, snapshots, and autoscaling. A 'Premium Dedicated Servers' tier starts at roughly $125 per month for stronger isolation, higher reliability guarantees, and priority support SLAs, with hybrid and fully dedicated enterprise arrangements available on request for larger deployments. Frappe Cloud offers a 14-day free trial, and Frappe Technologies also supports a network of more than 200 certified implementation partners globally who provide paid setup, customization, migration, and support services on top of either the self-hosted or cloud-hosted product; discounts are offered to educational institutions and registered nonprofits.

ERPNext's target audience is primarily small and mid-sized businesses (SMBs), manufacturers, and service-based organizations that need broad ERP functionality but are priced out of, or philosophically opposed to, per-user enterprise licensing from larger vendors. It is commonly adopted by manufacturers needing bill-of-materials, work-order, and shop-floor tracking; distributors and retailers needing multi-warehouse inventory and point-of-sale; professional services firms needing project and timesheet billing; and nonprofits or educational institutions drawn to its zero-license-cost self-hosted option. Because the underlying Frappe framework is a genuine low-code application platform, technically capable teams also use ERPNext as a base to build fully custom internal business applications beyond standard ERP scope.

In the competitive landscape, ERPNext is most frequently compared to Odoo, another modular, source-available ERP suite with a similarly broad app ecosystem; the two are considered the closest philosophical peers, both offering a free or low-cost path alongside a hosted commercial offering, though Odoo's core is only free for a single app and Odoo's enterprise edition uses per-user SaaS pricing, whereas ERPNext's self-hosted core remains fully free and unrestricted under GPLv3. At the higher end of the market, ERPNext is positioned as a lower-cost, more customizable alternative to proprietary mid-market and enterprise systems such as SAP Business One, Oracle NetSuite, and Microsoft Dynamics 365 Business Central, all of which typically charge $70 to $150 or more per user per month and require vendor-mediated customization. Among smaller open-source ERPs, Dolibarr is a lighter-weight comparable project, though it is generally regarded as less feature-complete for manufacturing and complex accounting than ERPNext. ERPNext's key differentiation across this set is the combination of a fully open, inspectable, and modifiable GPLv3 codebase; a metadata-driven low-code framework that makes deep customization possible without forking; and a hosting model priced on compute rather than headcount, which can make total cost of ownership substantially lower for organizations with many users.

Notable facts underline ERPNext's standing in the open-source ERP category: the frappe/erpnext repository on GitHub has accumulated roughly 36,000-plus stars and over 12,000 forks, reflecting one of the largest active open-source ERP communities in the world, and the project has been recognized in Gartner's ERP FrontRunners list as a Pacesetter. Frappe Technologies remains a relatively lean team, generally cited in the range of 50 to 200 employees, which is notable given the scope of the product and its global user base spanning thousands of organizations. The company has also fostered a broader open-source ecosystem in India through community initiatives such as FOSS United, and continues to expand its adjacent product line - including Frappe CRM, Frappe HR, Frappe Helpdesk, and Frappe LMS - all sharing the same underlying framework, positioning Frappe less as a single ERP vendor and more as a platform company for open-source business software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ERPNext is a free, open-source Enterprise Resource Planning system published by Frappe Technologies, a software company based in Mumbai, India. It brings accounting, inventory, manufacturing, purchasing, sales, CRM, HR and payroll, and project management together in a single connected application, so records flow between departments instead of living in separate disconnected tools.

The software is released under the GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3), meaning the full source code is publicly available on GitHub and can be self-hosted, inspected, and modified without license fees. Frappe Technologies also runs a managed hosting service, Frappe Cloud, for organizations that prefer not to operate the infrastructure themselves.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ERPNext is built on the Frappe framework, a Python and JavaScript based, metadata-driven web application framework that also powers sibling products like Frappe HR and Frappe CRM. Business objects are defined as 'DocTypes', which simultaneously generate a database table, a form, an API endpoint, and a permission model, allowing administrators to add custom fields and workflows without touching the core codebase.

The platform covers double-entry accounting and multi-currency ledgers, multi-warehouse inventory with batch and serial tracking, manufacturing with bills of materials and work orders, CRM and helpdesk pipelines, HR and payroll, project and timesheet billing, and point-of-sale, all accessible through a web interface, a REST API, and native mobile apps for iOS and Android.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ERPNext can be self-hosted for free under its GPLv3 license, with no per-user fees or module restrictions, provided the organization can operate its own servers. For teams that want managed hosting, Frappe Cloud prices by compute usage rather than by user seat, starting with a Sites plan from around $5 per month, a Servers/private bench plan from roughly $20 to $40 per month, and Premium Dedicated Servers from around $125 per month, with a 14-day free trial and discounts for education and nonprofit organizations.

Beyond hosting costs, organizations can also engage one of Frappe's 200-plus certified implementation partners for paid setup, customization, and migration services, which are priced separately from the software itself.

faqs.question: Is ERPNext really free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. ERPNext is released under the GPLv3 open-source license, so the self-hosted software itself has no license fee. Costs apply if you choose Frappe Cloud managed hosting or hire an implementation partner.

faqs.question: Who develops ERPNext?
faqs.answer: ERPNext is developed by Frappe Technologies, a company founded in 2008 by Rushabh Mehta and headquartered in Mumbai, India, on top of its own open-source Frappe framework.

faqs.question: What does Frappe Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Frappe Cloud's entry-level Sites plan starts at around $5 per month, with Servers plans from roughly $20 to $40 per month and Premium Dedicated Servers from around $125 per month, billed by compute usage rather than per user, plus a 14-day free trial.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ERPNext and the Frappe framework?
faqs.answer: The Frappe framework is the underlying Python and JavaScript application platform, while ERPNext is the specific ERP application built on top of it; the same framework also powers other Frappe products like Frappe HR and Frappe CRM.

faqs.question: How does ERPNext compare to Odoo?
faqs.answer: ERPNext and Odoo are the two most commonly compared open, modular ERP suites. ERPNext's self-hosted core remains fully free and unrestricted under GPLv3, while Odoo's free tier is limited to a single app and its full enterprise feature set uses per-user SaaS pricing.

faqs.question: Does ERPNext have a mobile app?

enrichment.short_description: Errbit is an open-source, self-hosted error-catching server built on Ruby on Rails and MongoDB that is API-compatible with Airbrake, letting teams collect and manage application exceptions on their own infrastructure.
enrichment.seo_title: Errbit Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Errbit review: a free, self-hosted, open-source error catcher built on Rails and MongoDB, compatible with the Airbrake API. Features, setup, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Errbit is an open-source, self-hosted error-catching and exception-monitoring server. It exists to give development teams the same kind of centralized error aggregation that hosted services like Airbrake provide, but running entirely on infrastructure the team owns and controls. Errbit collects exceptions reported by an application, groups related occurrences of the same underlying bug into a single error record, and presents them through a web dashboard so engineers can triage, assign, resolve, and track recurring problems across their applications and environments.

Technically, Errbit is built with Ruby on Rails on the application side and MongoDB as its data store, where all captured notices, apps, users, and comments are persisted. The project has evolved its MongoDB dependency over the years, with recent versions of the documentation specifying newer MongoDB releases (7.0 and above) as a requirement, reflecting continued maintenance of the codebase well past its original release. Deployment is typically done by teams already comfortable running and operating Rails applications, since Errbit is distributed as source code and, more conveniently, as an official Docker image on Docker Hub, which simplifies running it alongside a MongoDB instance in a self-managed environment. Configuration follows the twelve-factor app methodology, meaning nearly all runtime settings, from the MongoDB connection string to mail delivery and authentication providers, are supplied through environment variables rather than checked-in config files, which makes Errbit straightforward to deploy on container platforms.

The defining technical characteristic of Errbit is its Airbrake API compatibility. Airbrake (originally known as Hoptoad) popularized a simple HTTP-based protocol and a family of client notifier libraries for many languages, including Ruby, PHP, Python, Java, JavaScript, and others, that applications use to report unhandled exceptions to a central collector. Errbit implements the server side of that same protocol, so any application already instrumented with an Airbrake-compatible notifier gem or library can be pointed at a self-hosted Errbit server instead of the commercial Airbrake service simply by changing the API endpoint and key, with no changes needed to application code. This compatibility is what has made Errbit useful well beyond pure Ruby shops: because Airbrake notifier libraries exist for numerous ecosystems, Errbit can ingest errors from a polyglot set of services as long as each one uses an Airbrake-compatible client.

Once an error notice arrives, Errbit generates a fingerprint from attributes such as the error class, error message, full backtrace, the component or controller involved, the action, and the environment name. Notices that produce the same fingerprint are grouped and shown as repeated occurrences of one error, while notices with different fingerprints appear as distinct errors in the dashboard. This grouping logic is central to making the tool usable at scale, since a single bug in production can otherwise generate an overwhelming number of individual notices. Errbit also attaches contextual information to each notice, including timestamps, environment, request parameters, and, where the client library supports it, information about the specific end user who triggered the error, which helps teams reach out to affected users or prioritize fixes.

Errbit supports team workflows beyond simple error listing. It includes multiple authentication options for the dashboard itself, including GitHub OAuth, Google OAuth, and LDAP, so organizations can plug it into their existing identity systems rather than maintaining a separate credential store. It also integrates with issue trackers: when an application in Errbit is linked to a GitHub repository, users can create GitHub issues directly from an error record, and the project supports a plugin architecture (distributed as separate gems) for connecting to other issue trackers as well. Errbit even logs its own internal errors into an auto-created 'Self.Errbit' application inside itself, which is a small but telling detail about the maturity of its internal tooling.

Errbit is licensed under the permissive MIT License, which allows free use, modification, and redistribution, including in commercial contexts, with no royalty or seat-based fees of any kind. Because it is entirely open source and self-hosted, there is no vendor-provided pricing tier, trial period, or managed offering tied to the official project; the only ongoing cost is whatever infrastructure a team uses to run the Rails application and MongoDB database themselves. This makes total cost of ownership a function of hosting and operations rather than a subscription, which is attractive to teams with strict data residency requirements, cost sensitivity at high error volumes, or a general preference for owning their observability data outright.

Errbit originated as a community-built, open-source alternative to the hosted Airbrake service, growing out of the broader Ruby community's interest in the twelve-factor, self-hosted software movement of the early 2010s. Its GitHub repository under the errbit organization has been active since 2010 and has accumulated thousands of stars and hundreds of forks over more than a decade, along with a long list of external contributors, reflecting sustained, community-driven maintenance rather than a single vendor's roadmap. The project has continued to release incremental versions, documented from v0.1.0 through more recent 0.11.x releases and an active main development branch, updating its Ruby, Rails, and MongoDB compatibility as those underlying dependencies have evolved.

The target users for Errbit are engineering teams, typically at startups, agencies, or organizations with in-house DevOps or platform expertise, who want centralized exception tracking without sending error data (which can include stack traces, request parameters, and sometimes user identifiers) to a third-party SaaS vendor. It is particularly well suited to teams already running Ruby on Rails applications who want a tool that shares the same technology stack and operational patterns they already know, though its Airbrake API compatibility means it can also serve as a lightweight error-collection backend for services written in other languages.

In the competitive landscape, Errbit sits alongside both the commercial service it was built to replace, Airbrake, and a wider set of exception-monitoring products including Sentry, Rollbar, Bugsnag, and Honeybadger. Those alternatives are primarily hosted SaaS platforms (some, like Sentry, also offer self-hosted or open-source editions) with broader language support, richer alerting integrations, performance monitoring, and release-tracking features layered on top of basic error capture. Errbit differentiates itself by being narrowly focused, fully open source under a permissive license, and simple to self-host, trading some of the polish and breadth of managed competitors for full data control, zero licensing cost, and straightforward Rails-based operability. For teams that specifically need an Airbrake-protocol-compatible, self-managed error collector and are comfortable operating a Rails and MongoDB stack, Errbit remains a well-established and still-maintained option within the open-source error-tracking space.

paragraphs: Errbit is an open-source, self-hosted error-catching server designed to collect and manage exceptions reported by other applications. It is built with Ruby on Rails and stores all captured error data in MongoDB, giving teams a self-managed alternative to hosted error-monitoring services.

The defining feature of Errbit is that it is Airbrake API compliant. Any application already instrumented with an Airbrake-compatible notifier library, across Ruby, PHP, Python, Java, JavaScript, and other ecosystems, can send its exceptions to a self-hosted Errbit server simply by pointing the client at a new endpoint and API key, without changing application code.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Errbit groups incoming error notices into fingerprinted error records based on the error class, message, backtrace, component, action, and environment, so recurring occurrences of the same bug appear as one item rather than flooding the dashboard with duplicates.

Beyond core error capture, Errbit supports GitHub, Google, and LDAP authentication for dashboard access, can create GitHub issues directly from an error record when an app is linked to a repository, and follows twelve-factor configuration principles so nearly every setting is controlled through environment variables, which suits containerized deployment with the official Docker image.

paragraphs: Errbit is released under the permissive MIT License and is entirely free to use, modify, and redistribute, including for commercial purposes. There is no vendor subscription, seat fee, or usage-based charge from the project itself.

The only real cost is infrastructure: teams need to run the Rails application and a MongoDB database somewhere, whether on their own servers, a container platform, or a cloud provider, so total cost of ownership depends entirely on self-hosting choices rather than a pricing tier.

faqs.answer: Errbit is an open-source, self-hosted error-catching server built with Ruby on Rails and MongoDB that collects and manages exceptions reported by applications, functioning as a self-hosted alternative to the Airbrake service.

faqs.question: Is Errbit free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Errbit is released under the MIT License, so it can be downloaded, self-hosted, modified, and used commercially at no cost. The only expense is the infrastructure needed to run it.

faqs.answer: Yes. Because Errbit implements the Airbrake API, applications using any Airbrake-compatible notifier library, including those for PHP, Python, Java, and JavaScript, can send error notices to a self-hosted Errbit server.

faqs.question: How does Errbit group errors?
faqs.answer: Errbit generates a fingerprint for each incoming notice based on the error class, message, full backtrace, component or controller, action, and environment name, so repeated occurrences of the same underlying bug are grouped together in the dashboard.

faqs.question: How does Errbit compare to Airbrake, Sentry, Rollbar, Bugsnag, and Honeybadger?
faqs.answer: Airbrake, Sentry, Rollbar, Bugsnag, and Honeybadger are primarily hosted commercial error-monitoring services with broader feature sets. Errbit differentiates itself by being fully open source, free under the MIT License, and designed specifically to be self-hosted while remaining compatible with Airbrake's client libraries.

enrichment.short_description: ESMira is a free, open-source, self-hosted platform for running Experience Sampling Method and ambulatory assessment studies, pairing native Android and iOS survey apps with a researcher-controlled PHP server.
enrichment.headquarters: Krems an der Donau, Austria (Karl Landsteiner University of Health Sciences)
enrichment.seo_title: ESMira Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ESMira review: an open-source, self-hosted experience sampling platform for researchers. See features, licensing, pricing and academic alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
ESMira is an open-source software platform for conducting Experience Sampling Method (ESM), Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA), and ambulatory assessment research, in which participants complete short repeated surveys in their everyday lives via a smartphone app. It was developed by David Lewetz and Stefan Stieger at the Department of Psychology and Psychodynamics, Karl Landsteiner University of Health Sciences in Krems an der Donau, Austria, with development beginning in early 2019 and continuing since. The project and its methodology are described in a peer-reviewed paper, 'ESMira: A decentralized open-source application for collecting experience sampling data,' published in Behavior Research Methods in August 2023.

Technically, ESMira consists of two parts: a PHP-based server application that a study administrator installs on their own webspace, and native mobile apps for Android and iOS (plus a web-based fallback) that participants use to receive prompts and complete questionnaires. Because the server is self-hosted by the researcher rather than run by a third-party company, all collected data stays on infrastructure the research team controls, and participant identity is handled through randomly generated, anonymous user identifiers rather than accounts tied to personal information. The mobile apps can store completed surveys locally and sync once a connection is available, so a permanent internet connection is not required during data collection. The full source code is released under the GNU Affero General Public License (AGPL-3.0) and is hosted on GitHub under the KL-Psychological-Methodology organization, with an accompanying wiki and issue tracker for community support.

Features documented in the academic paper and project materials include support for complex, nested time-based sampling schedules, an anonymous built-in chat channel for administrators to message participants without breaking anonymity, a reward-code incentive system that allows compensation without linking payments to identity, and live graphical feedback showing participants their own response statistics over time. ESMira targets academic researchers in psychology and the social sciences who need a no-cost, data-sovereign alternative to commercial ambulatory assessment platforms such as movisensXS, PIEL Survey, mEMA, Ethica Data, m-Path, and RealLife Exp, or to other research-oriented tools like ExpiWell, RADAR-base, and SEMA3. Its main differentiation from these commercial and semi-commercial platforms is the fully decentralized, self-hosted, and free model, at the cost of requiring the study administrator to have access to a PHP-enabled webserver rather than relying on a managed cloud service. Given its academic origin and modest scale, ESMira's public footprint is limited to its official site, GitHub repository, and the associated Behavior Research Methods publication rather than broad company or commercial documentation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ESMira is an open-source software system built specifically for Experience Sampling Method, Ecological Momentary Assessment, and ambulatory assessment research. It was created by David Lewetz and Stefan Stieger of the Department of Psychology and Psychodynamics at Karl Landsteiner University of Health Sciences in Austria, with development starting in early 2019 and the underlying methodology described in a 2023 paper published in Behavior Research Methods.

The system is split into a self-hosted PHP server, which researchers install on their own webspace to design studies and collect data, and native Android and iOS apps (with a web interface as an alternative) that participants use to answer prompted questionnaires. Because the server runs on infrastructure the researcher controls rather than a vendor's cloud, ESMira is described in its own documentation as decentralized, and participant data collection and communication are designed to stay anonymous throughout a study.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ESMira supports complex, nested time-based sampling schedules, which lets researchers design studies with multiple prompt types and timing rules within a single project. It includes an anonymous, built-in chat feature so study administrators can message participants without collecting identifying contact information, and a reward-code system that allows incentivizing participation while preserving anonymity.

Participants can see live graphical feedback and personal statistics as they progress through a study, which is intended to support engagement over the course of longer sampling periods. The mobile apps can store completed surveys locally and upload them once connectivity returns, so continuous internet access is not required to participate.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ESMira is free and open source, released under the GNU Affero General Public License (AGPL-3.0). There is no commercial pricing tier: the source code, server software, and mobile apps are all available at no cost from the project's GitHub repository, and researchers run their own instance on a PHP-enabled webserver they provide themselves.

Because ESMira is self-hosted rather than offered as a managed SaaS product, the only practical cost to a research team is the webserver hosting they already need to supply, not a subscription or license fee to the ESMira project itself.
